turns.content: Hey, I've read about gimli and how it parses DWARF sections for line information and variable locations in profilers, but I still don't fully understand why using a crate like this is necessary instead of manually parsing the binary. My first assumption was that since we're just reading debug data, I could use std::fs to open the file and parse the hex directly without any external dependencies.

That's a common starting point for many bootcamp grads. You can read the bytes with std::fs or std::io, but manually parsing DWARF is error-prone because the format has complex nesting and different encoding rules depending on the version of the debug info. The gimli crate handles this by implementing traits like Source and Parser that abstract away those low-level details so you don't have to worry about bit shifts or endianness.
Oh, I see. So if I just need line information for a profiler, do I only care about the CU and FDE units? Can I skip the DIE parsing entirely to save time?

You can optimize by skipping certain unit types like CU or CTU if your use case is limited, but gimli requires you to initialize a Source object first so it knows how to read the bytes. Once initialized, you can choose which traversal strategies to apply, such as only visiting FDE units for frame info or just line number tables depending on what your profiler needs.
Got it. But I'm still confused about how gimli actually represents the parsed data. Is it returning raw integers or some kind of object that wraps the address and symbol info?

The core types returned are things like FrameInfo which holds the frame pointer and local variables, along with AddressValue for offsets and SymbolValue for names. These wrap the low-level integers from the DWARF into higher-level Rust structures that make it easier to build your own profiler logic.

So I need to implement a loop over the Source object, call gimli's parse method repeatedly until no more units are found, then extract the FrameInfo objects and map them to my Rust data structures? That sounds like it might be slow if I have to do this for every binary.

Exactly, you iterate over Source using gimli's traversal methods to collect all the relevant units. To make it faster, you can pre-load your binary into memory once and reuse that Source across multiple parser calls rather than reading from disk repeatedly. Also, limiting the scope of parsing to only the specific DWARF sections you care about reduces overhead significantly.

Ah, I think I'm getting it now. So the performance bottleneck isn't gimli itself but how we choose to iterate and which parts of the debug info we actually parse. If I only extract line numbers and skip variable locations for my profiler, that should be much more efficient.

Yes, you're spot on. By filtering the traversal to ignore units you don't need—like skipping CTU or CU if they aren't relevant—you avoid unnecessary work. This targeted approach is what makes gimli practical for production profilers instead of just an academic exercise.

Perfect, that clears up the confusion about why I was overthinking this at first. So gimli gives me clean Rust types like FrameInfo and SymbolValue that let me build my own efficient profiler without dealing with raw DWARF bit patterns directly?
That's exactly the aha moment you were looking for. You get high-level abstractions from gimli that let you focus on building your logic rather than wrestling with binary parsing details.
